learning_outcomes.description: Understand the dynamics that move states to cooperate (or compete) in international organisations.
Identify the respective mandates and objectives of international organisations in world politics.
Understand the concepts and theoretical positions that apply regarding cooperation, collective actors and the constitution of common interests in world politics.
Demonstrate skill in articulating complex ideas and issues of global and regional politics in a logical and coherent manner.
Demonstrate knowledge and skill to appreciate and analyse some of the most compelling challenges that international organisations faces in the contemporary world.

assessments.task_description:
1 mark per session over 10 weeks from Week 2 to Week 12. No seminar on Week 10 due to a public holiday.

Assessment will be based on in-class seminar contributions, including participation in activities, group work, and discussions.

Instructions and rubrics will be made available to students on Blackboard.

In the event of a seminar absence, please contact the course coordinator to discuss your circumstances.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Online Submission via Turnitin.

In Week 3 and Week 11, students are required to submit tasks designed to support mid-term essay and end-of-semester exam preparation.

Each task is worth 5% of the final grade.

These tasks may include identifying primary and secondary sources and critically analysing the required readings.

Instructions and rubrics will be made available to students on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Online Submission via Turnitin.

Word count: 2,500 words (+/- 10% leeway), excluding notes and bibliography.

Essay Questions, instructions and rubrics will be made available to students on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In-person exam during the end-of-semester examination period.

Instructions and rubrics will be made available to students on Blackboard.

The exam type is an Essay Exam. Students will be asked to write a long answer for the question in essay form, with introductory and concluding paragraphs to ensure a logical structure. Exam tips for Essay Exams are available at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/student-support/study-skills-and-learning-advice/study-skills-and-learning-advice-overview/exam-tips

The exam is digital and requires students to bring their own device. Students can contact the library to borrow a laptop and to seek support for any technical issues. Further information is available at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/digital-examinations
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction

  • Welcome
  • The transnational nature of contemporary crises
  • A burgeoning nongovernmental sector and new actors
  • Key terms: international organisation, international institution, international regime, and global governance 
  • Course Overview: Objectives, structure, and assessment
  • Course staff and office hours

Theories

  • Theories as conceptual tools and alternative attitudes for understanding international organisations
  • Realism
  • Liberalism
  • Constructivism
  • Marxism
  • The ontology of international organisations
  • Organisation as an actor, resource, and forum

learning_activities.topic:
History

  • Balance-of-power policies and the concept of collective security
  • The League of Nations and its response to two major crises
  • The UN collective security system (e.g., the Korean War)
  • The evolution of UN preventive diplomacy and peacekeeping operations

learning_activities.topic:
United Nations

  • “The United Nations is dead”?
  • UN’s goals and complexity: why it is difficult to define what the UN is and does
  • UN structure
  • Principal organs: the Security Council, the General Assembly
  • Funds and programs
  • Functions: Peace and security, human rights, sustainable development
  • Ongoing challenges

  • The EU’s role in global governance
